sources.name: FRED
sources.label: Federal Reserve Economic Data
sources.note: Public CSV downloads from FRED graph endpoints; no API key required for the graph CSV route.

sources.name: Multpl
sources.label: Multpl valuation tables
sources.note: Public HTML tables for long-history S&P 500 valuation ratios such as Shiller CAPE, PE, earnings yield, and dividend yield.

sources.name: Siblis Research
sources.label: Siblis index valuation tables
sources.note: Public index valuation tables used for Russell 2000 PE, forward PE, and CAPE history.

sources.name: Yahoo Finance Chart
sources.label: Yahoo market chart endpoint
sources.note: Used only for optional live market series where FRED coverage is weak. The app degrades gracefully if blocked.

sources.name: U.S. Treasury FiscalData
sources.label: Treasury Fiscal Data API
sources.note: Open public REST API; reserved for extension points and source attribution.

sources.name: World Bank
sources.label: World Bank open data API
sources.note: Public country-level annual macro indicators including GDP growth, CPI inflation, unemployment, and current-account balances.

scenarios.name: Valuation fragility
scenarios.description: Equity valuation and bubble pressure across CAPE, PE, earnings yield gaps, profits, relative tech leadership, and policy-rate pressure.

scenarios.name: Bubble breadth / AI leadership
scenarios.description: Report-informed dot-com style checklist: tech leadership, narrow breadth proxies, valuation cushions, real-rate pressure, and low-vol complacency.

scenarios.name: Cross-asset risk-off
scenarios.description: Market stress visible in volatility, credit spreads, equities, dollar strength, oil stress, and curve behavior.

scenarios.name: Sovereign refinancing
scenarios.description: Country-level debt fragility across government debt loads, long-term bond yields, and sovereign spread pressure.

Canada general government gross debt to GDP

scenarios.name: Inflation shock
scenarios.description: Price pressure impulse from realized inflation, breakevens, oil, gasoline, natural gas, and inflation expectations.

scenarios.name: Liquidity drain
scenarios.description: Balance-sheet and reserve pressure from Fed QT, reserve balances, TGA, RRP, broad dollar strength, and high front-end rates.

scenarios.name: Growth scare
scenarios.description: Real-economy deterioration: labor stress, production weakness, retail softness, housing slowdown, and survey contraction.

scenarios.name: JPY carry unwind
scenarios.description: Yen funding trade pressure: yen strength, equity volatility, Nikkei stress, U.S. rate compression, and broad risk-off.

drivers.name: Japan minus U.S. 10-year government bond spread

scenarios.name: Funding stress
scenarios.description: Bank funding and credit pressure across financial conditions, OAS, money-market rates, volatility, and Fed liquidity plumbing.

drivers.name: Equity market value to Fed assets

scenarios.name: External vulnerability
scenarios.description: Balance-of-payments and FX pressure from current-account weakness, dollar strength, and country-specific external stress.

scenarios.name: Country macro shock
scenarios.description: Large-country macro deterioration across growth, inflation, labor markets, and domestic policy stress.

caseStudies.name: Dot-com / AI analogue
caseStudies.market: NASDAQ peak before the 2000-2002 equity unwind
caseStudies.summary: The report-informed question set is intentionally narrow: equity valuation and tech leadership were extreme, while classic credit and labor stress were not the main early warning at the peak.
caseStudies.lesson: Treat it as a fragility checklist, not a crash timer. The dangerous setup is valuation plus narrow leadership plus tightening/low risk premium; the catalyst can arrive later.
caseStudies.reportBasis: Local 2000 reports were used only to extract candidate questions: valuation cushion, risk premia, real rates, profit expectations, internet-sector P/E and P/S extremes, IPO/new-issue pressure, and dispersion across internet subsectors.
caseStudies.currentRead: Recent reports are treated the same way: they suggest questions about breadth, AI/semi leadership, positioning, earnings expectations, IPO appetite, oil/inflation tails, and left-tail hedging. Public data decides whether those questions are active.

pillars.name: Valuation cushion
pillars.reportRead: 2000 work asked whether the new economy justified elevated valuations; useful signals are CAPE, PE, dividend/earnings yield, and market-cap-to-GDP style ratios.
pillars.todayRead: Checks whether current valuation leaves little cushion if AI earnings or discount-rate assumptions wobble.

pillars.name: Leadership and breadth
pillars.reportRead: Internet reports show extreme sector multiples and sharp internet/technology leadership; recent reports call out breadth near dot-com-like narrowness.
pillars.todayRead: Uses public proxies for tech dominance and momentum: NASDAQ relative performance, NASDAQ one-year momentum, and semiconductors versus the market.

pillars.name: Profit assumptions
pillars.reportRead: The 2000 bullish case depended on realized earnings growth and improved profit expectations; that makes profit growth and margins part of the bubble diagnosis.
pillars.todayRead: Flags whether current equity strength rests on unusually strong earnings growth or high aggregate margins.

pillars.name: Rates and risk premia
pillars.reportRead: The new-economy valuation case explicitly used lower risk premia and lower real rates; Fed tightening is the pressure point when that assumption reverses.
pillars.todayRead: Compares today with the 2000 setup through real policy rates, recent Fed tightening, the curve, and long-history credit spreads.

pillars.name: Complacency and tails
pillars.reportRead: A bubble can keep rising while volatility is quiet; low-vol regimes become more dangerous when valuation and leadership signals are already stretched.
pillars.todayRead: Recent desk reports mention low implied volatility, downside demand, and oil/inflation tails; this pillar separates complacency from already-realized stress.

metrics.name: NASDAQ Composite relative to S&P 500
metrics.why: A high NASDAQ/S&P ratio shows tech and growth leadership becoming unusually dominant versus the broad large-cap market, a core dot-com style signal.

metrics.name: Semiconductor leadership relative to S&P 500
metrics.why: Semiconductor outperformance is a cleaner current-cycle proxy for AI infrastructure leadership and potential narrow-market crowding.

metrics.name: NASDAQ Composite one-year price momentum
metrics.why: Fast one-year NASDAQ appreciation flags speculative momentum and crowded growth leadership.

metrics.name: Buffett indicator proxy
metrics.why: A public-data Buffett indicator proxy: nonfinancial corporate equity market value divided by nominal GDP.

metrics.name: S&P 500 Shiller CAPE ratio
metrics.why: CAPE compares equity prices with a 10-year smoothed earnings base; very high readings flag expensive long-cycle equity valuation.

metrics.name: S&P 500 dividend yield
metrics.why: Low dividend yield is a simple long-history valuation pressure gauge.

metrics.name: 10Y minus 2Y Treasury spread
metrics.why: Deep inversion or sudden steepening can flag recession risk or policy panic.

metrics.name: Moody's Baa corporate bond spread to 10-year Treasury
metrics.why: Baa-Treasury spread is a long-history credit risk-premium check; quiet credit alongside extreme equity valuation suggests bubble fragility rather than funding crisis.

metrics.name: WTI crude three-month price shock
metrics.why: Recent reports highlight oil and energy shocks as a macro risk that can collide with equity optimism through inflation and margins.

metrics.name: Corporate profits after tax to GDP
metrics.why: High corporate profits relative to GDP indicate unusually rich aggregate margins; that can support valuations but also raises mean-reversion risk.

metrics.name: Real earnings yield gap
metrics.why: Compares equity earnings yield with real Treasury yield; low readings flag valuation fragility when real rates are high.

metrics.name: S&P 500 real earnings growth
metrics.why: The 2000 new-economy argument leaned on very strong earnings and profit expectations; extreme earnings growth can either justify valuations temporarily or become a mean-reversion risk.

metrics.name: Fed model earnings yield gap
metrics.why: Compares S&P 500 earnings yield with the 10-year Treasury yield; low or negative gaps mean equities offer less yield cushion versus bonds.

metrics.name: S&P 500 trailing PE ratio
metrics.why: Trailing PE captures how much investors pay for realized earnings; high readings can make markets more fragile to rate or earnings shocks.

metrics.name: Six-month Fed funds tightening impulse
metrics.why: Rapid Fed tightening is a direct pressure point for high-duration and speculative equities.

metrics.name: S&P 500 earnings yield
metrics.why: Low earnings yield means investors receive little earnings compensation for equity risk.

metrics.name: Low VIX complacency gauge
metrics.why: Unusually low implied volatility can mean investors are underpaying for left-tail risk while valuation or positioning risk is building.

metrics.name: 10-year Treasury yield
metrics.why: The long rate anchors global discount rates; fast moves in either direction can shock positioning.

metrics.name: CBOE VIX
metrics.why: Equity implied volatility is the fastest public proxy for forced de-risking.

metrics.name: 10-year breakeven inflation
metrics.why: Market-implied inflation compensation can warn before realized inflation data updates.

metrics.name: Real effective Fed funds rate
metrics.why: A rising real policy rate can prick long-duration equity stories by making funding and discount rates less forgiving.
